---
title: "4.10 Add and Remove Elements"
subtitle: "Implementing ArrayList Algorithms"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Implementing ArrayList Algorithms

### Standard `ArrayList` algorithms 1/2

There are standard `ArrayList` algorithms that utilize traversals to:

- insert elements
- delete elements
- determine a minimum or maximum value
- compute a sum or average
- determine if at least one element has a particular property
- determine if all elements have a particular property

## Implementing ArrayList Algorithms

### Standard `ArrayList` algorithms 2/2

There are standard `ArrayList` algorithms that utilize traversals to:

- determine the number of elements having a particular property
- access all consecutive pairs of elements
- determine the presence or absence of duplicate elements
- shift or rotate elements left or right
- reverse the order of the elements

## Common Traversal Loops

### Enhanced `for` and indexed `for`

Here are two common `ArrayList` traversal loops that can be used for these algorithms.

```java
for (Type obj : list)
{
    if (condition about obj)
    {
        // body for matching values
    }
}

for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)
{
    if (condition about list.get(i))
    {
        // body for matching indices
    }
}
```

## Add/Remove Elements

### Trace basic `ArrayList` methods

You should be able to trace through code that uses all the basic `ArrayList` methods.

Key methods in this lesson:

- `add(obj)`
- `add(index, obj)`
- `set(index, obj)`
- `remove(index)`

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qListRem3`

What will print when the following code executes?

```java
List<Integer> numList = new ArrayList<Integer>();
numList.add(new Integer(1));
numList.add(new Integer(2));
numList.add(new Integer(3));
numList.set(2, new Integer(4));
numList.add(1, new Integer(5));
numList.add(new Integer(6));
System.out.println(numList);
```

## Quick Check: Options

### qListRem3

Options:

A. `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`  
B. `[1, 2, 4, 5, 6]`  
C. `[1, 2, 5, 4, 6]`  
D. `[1, 5, 2, 4, 6]`

## Quick Check: Answer

### qListRem3 reasoning

Correct answer: **D. `[1, 5, 2, 4, 6]`**

`add` without an index adds at the end.

`set` replaces the item at that index.

`add` with an index moves all current values at that index or beyond to the right.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qListRem4`

What will print when the following code executes?

```java
List<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<Integer>();
list1.add(new Integer(1));
list1.add(new Integer(2));
list1.add(new Integer(3));
list1.remove(1);
System.out.println(list1);
```

Options:

A. `[2, 3]`  
B. `[1, 2, 3]`  
C. `[1, 2]`  
D. `[1, 3]`

## Quick Check: Answer

### qListRem4 reasoning

Correct answer: **D. `[1, 3]`**

The item at index `1` is removed and the `3` is moved left.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: arrayListRemoveInLoop`

The following code is supposed to initialize the `ArrayList` `arr` to `[0,1,2,3,4]` and then remove every other element to get `[1,3]`.

However, when you remove an element the size of the array changes and elements move up an index.

Trace through the code and figure out why you get the unexpected result.

## arrayListRemoveInLoop Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class ArrayListLoop
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> arr = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
        {
            arr.add(i);
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < arr.size(); i++)
        {
            if (i % 2 == 0)
            {
                System.out.println("Removing element " + i + " : " + arr.get(i));
                arr.remove(i);
            }
        }
        System.out.println(arr);
    }
}
```
:::

## arrayListRemoveInLoop Test Target

### Runestone expected output

The Runestone test checks the output from `main`.

```text
Removing element 0: 0
Removing element 2: 3
[1, 2, 4]
```

The source expects this unexpected result, because the exercise is about tracing the removal bug.

## Remove While Traversing

### Source note

If you use `add` or `remove` inside a loop that traverses an `ArrayList`, you may get unexpected results because the size of the `ArrayList` has changed.

Enhanced `for` should not be used to remove elements from an `ArrayList`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

- distinguish `add(obj)`, `add(index, obj)`, `set(index, obj)`, and `remove(index)`
- trace index shifts after an insertion or removal
- explain why removing while moving forward can skip values
- explain why enhanced `for` should not be used to remove elements
- preserve the source expected output for `arrayListRemoveInLoop`

## End

### 4.10 Add and Remove Elements

Next: Min, Max, Sum, Average, and Finding a Property.
